jokes.text: Everything on planes is tiny. Tiny food, tiny liquor bottles, tiny pillows...tiny bathroom, tiny sink, tiny soap. Everyone's in a cramped seat working on a tiny computer.
jokes.punchline: The escalating repetition of 'tiny' everything creates absurd emphasis on airline miniaturization
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld observational comedy with rhythmic repetition that builds momentum. The word 'tiny' becomes absurdly overused, which is the joke.

jokes.text: There'll be a man in a tight suit. He'll tell you you have little time...to make your connecting flight. So move it.
jokes.punchline: The contradiction between 'little time' and the casual 'So move it' after detailed explanation
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor but not particularly memorable or original.

jokes.text: Hey, could you do me a favor? Could you shut up?
jokes.punchline: Subverts expectation with rude demand disguised as favor
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with economic delivery. The politeness makes the rudeness funnier.

jokes.text: Fine, I'll take it off. Grab the wheel.
jokes.punchline: Casual request for passenger to drive while he undresses
jokes.explanation: Absurd casual attitude toward dangerous situation creates humor through understatement.

jokes.text: My hand is stuck...My hand! My hand!...Don't... Jer... All right, fine.
jokes.punchline: Escalating panic as simple task goes wrong
jokes.explanation: Generic physical comedy moment. Works but not particularly clever or memorable.

jokes.text: And it smells like a cheap hooker. Or is that you? Give me 10 bucks and find out.
jokes.setup: Jerry complains about rental car smell
jokes.punchline: Elaine's quick comeback turning insult into proposition
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that shows Elaine's wit. The specificity of '$10' makes it funnier than a generic retort.

jokes.text: He didn't come within 2 feet of it. He's waving at it.
jokes.setup: George failed to touch the awning
jokes.punchline: The visual of George pathetically waving at unreachable awning
jokes.explanation: Perfect George characterization - the image of him waving at the awning he can't reach is both pathetic and hilarious.

jokes.text: People like you? Not much. You don't know what you're doing.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks what tip people like them usually give
jokes.punchline: Handler's brutally honest assessment of their incompetence
jokes.explanation: Unexpected honesty from service worker character. The casual brutality of the assessment is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Usually, I get $5 a bag...That's right...Five dollars a bag? I don't think so.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's indignant rejection of the answer he specifically requested
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry behavior - asking a question then being outraged by the honest answer. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Never be late for a plane with a girl. Because a girl runs like a girl, with the little steps and the arms flailing out.
jokes.setup: Jerry's observation about running with women
jokes.punchline: Detailed description of stereotypical female running style
jokes.explanation: Dated gender stereotype humor that relies on obvious observation rather than clever insight.

jokes.text: You wanna make this plane, you gotta run like a man. Get your knees up!
jokes.punchline: Jerry coaching Elaine's running technique mid-sprint
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy continues the running gag. The urgency mixed with coaching is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: Well...I'll take the first class.
jokes.setup: One first class, one coach seat available
jokes.punchline: Jerry's immediate, shameless claim on the better seat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry characterization - his selfishness is so casual and expected it becomes funny.

jokes.text: Elaine, have you ever flown first class? No. All right, then. You won't know what you're missing.
jokes.punchline: Twisted logic that ignorance makes the worse option better
jokes.explanation: Brilliant twisted logic that's simultaneously selfish and almost reasonable. Classic Seinfeld rationalization.

jokes.text: I've flown first class, Elaine. I can't go back to coach. I can't. I won't.
jokes.punchline: Dramatic declaration about his inability to 'go back' to coach
jokes.explanation: The dramatic 'I can't. I won't.' about airplane seating is absurdly over-the-top for the situation.

jokes.text: The plane crashes, everybody in first class is gonna die anyway. Yeah, I'm sure you'll live.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's sarcastic response about her survival chances
jokes.explanation: Dark humor followed by quick sarcastic comeback. Good back-and-forth dynamic.

jokes.text: Bon voyage, Lainey.
jokes.setup: Jerry leaving Elaine for first class
jokes.punchline: Pretentious farewell as if departing for different destinations
jokes.explanation: Jerry's pretentious goodbye treats the class difference like a transatlantic voyage.

jokes.text: I never check my bags. Can't stand that waiting in the baggage area. Great.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's flat 'Great' response to unwanted conversation
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe comedy from unwanted airplane conversation. Elaine's deadpan response works.

jokes.text: Help me.
jokes.punchline: Direct plea to audience/camera for rescue
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective expression of social imprisonment. Economy of words makes it work.

jokes.text: I confused it with another awning.
jokes.setup: Kramer asking why George thought he could touch the awning
jokes.punchline: George's absurd excuse that he mixed up awnings
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - the absurd specificity of confusing awnings is exactly how his mind works.

jokes.text: I cannot express to you the feeling I get from a perfect pickup.
jokes.punchline: Treating mundane pickup as profound achievement requiring expression
jokes.explanation: George's obsessive pride in trivial accomplishments perfectly characterized through overblown language.

jokes.text: This is a suicide mission! I had it perfectly timed out.
jokes.setup: Kramer taking wrong route to airport
jokes.punchline: George's dramatic overreaction to minor route change
jokes.explanation: George's tendency toward dramatic catastrophizing is well-established character trait.

jokes.text: Duty-free is the biggest sucker deal in retail. You know how much duty is? Duty's nothing. It's like sales tax.
jokes.setup: Kramer wants to visit duty-free shop
jokes.punchline: George's passionate rant deflating the concept of duty-free savings
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational comedy that deflates a common travel assumption. George's intensity sells it.

jokes.text: I like to stop At the duty-free shop
jokes.punchline: Kramer's simple, unmoved repetition of his desire
jokes.explanation: Kramer's obliviousness to logic and facts is perfectly displayed through simple repetition.

jokes.text: So he says, 'Squeeze your breasts together.' And I say, 'I thought this was an ad for shoes.'
jokes.punchline: Disconnect between breast-focused direction and shoe advertising
jokes.explanation: Solid setup/punchline about absurd advertising practices. Good structure but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Good thing they gave you that washcloth to cover yourself up. What is this an ad for? See those wrinkled jeans slung over the chair? Way in the background, out of focus?
jokes.punchline: Product being advertised is barely visible in background while model dominates
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about advertising absurdity. The specific details make it work.

jokes.text: He's sleeping, and I have to go to the bathroom. Maybe he'll wake up soon. What if my kidneys burst? Is it worth it not to wake this man up to damage a major organ?
jokes.setup: Elaine needs bathroom but passenger is sleeping
jokes.punchline: Escalating from minor inconvenience to organ damage
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from politeness to medical emergency. Relatable social anxiety humor.

jokes.text: I hope this disgusting slob appreciates what I'm doing for him.
jokes.punchline: Her resentment builds as she calls him a 'disgusting slob'
jokes.explanation: Natural progression of resentment. Not exceptional but fits the character arc.

jokes.text: Look at you. You run like a girl. Come on. Run like a man. Lift your knees.
jokes.setup: Callback to Jerry's earlier running commentary
jokes.punchline: George now coaching Kramer's running technique
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier running joke but without adding new elements. Functional but uninspired.

jokes.text: Do I still get credit for the pickup? I was here.
jokes.punchline: George's concern about getting credit despite circumstances beyond control
jokes.explanation: Perfect George obsession with meaningless achievements and getting proper credit.

jokes.text: Gee, I'm sorry. I got here first. I don't care. I want the magazine.
jokes.punchline: Convict's immediate hostile response to politeness
jokes.explanation: Sets up conflict well but the setup itself isn't particularly funny.

jokes.text: You know what I would do to you if I wasn't in these shackles? But you are, Blanche. You are in the shackles.
jokes.punchline: Kramer's movie reference response to genuine threat
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kramer moment - treating serious threat as opportunity for movie quote. 'Whatever Happened to Baby Jane?' reference is perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, I can't wait to read my TIME magazine. Last copy too. Maybe I'll read it tomorrow, in the park. Supposed to be a beautiful day. Have a nice life...sentence, that is.
jokes.punchline: Building to 'life sentence' pun while taunting convict
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with pun payoff. Kramer's obliviousness to danger makes his taunting funnier.

jokes.text: Wake up, you human slug. Wake up! Wake up! I can't hold it anymore.
jokes.punchline: Finally snapping and calling sleeping passenger 'human slug'
jokes.explanation: Satisfying payoff to building tension. 'Human slug' is a good specific insult.

jokes.text: Why, it's a perfect fit. You must be Cinderella.
jokes.punchline: Flight attendant's flirty Cinderella reference
jokes.explanation: Mild flirtation humor. Works but not particularly memorable or clever.

jokes.text: He took the cash and...disappears. I tried to find him. I went to his girlfriend's house, even his family. Never got that money back. He screwed me.
jokes.punchline: Detailed account of being screwed over for $240 twenty years ago
jokes.explanation: Sets up Kramer's motivation but not inherently funny. More plot advancement than comedy.

jokes.text: Kramer, you cannot abandon people in an airport pickup. It's a binding social contract. We must go forward. Not back.
jokes.setup: Kramer wants to abandon pickup for revenge
jokes.punchline: George treating pickup as sacred 'binding social contract'
jokes.explanation: Perfect George pomposity - elevating mundane pickup to philosophical principle with dramatic language.

jokes.text: It's like an English garden in there. They're gardenias, mostly. I thought I smelled lilac. Yes, there are a few of those too. It's almost overwhelming.
jokes.punchline: Treating airplane bathroom like botanical paradise
jokes.explanation: Absurd contrast between airplane reality and luxury description. Shows first-class pretension well.

jokes.text: Let's go. Listen to the bell, Grossbard. It tolls for thee.
jokes.punchline: Dramatic literary reference (Hemingway/Donne) for $240 debt
jokes.explanation: Excellent literary pretension from Kramer for petty revenge. The gravity mismatched to the offense is perfect.

jokes.text: Oh, saffron. That sounds good. And today we're featuring wines from the Tuscany region. Tuscany. Tuscany.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dreamy repetition of 'Tuscany' as if it's magical
jokes.explanation: Mild satire of first-class pretension. Jerry's enchantment with the word 'Tuscany' is mildly amusing.

jokes.text: You're not supposed to get up during the food service. Well, nobody told me that. Look. This plane is full. I got a lot of people to serve. Now, please. You're just gonna have to wait.
jokes.setup: Elaine trying to return to seat
jokes.punchline: Coach flight attendant's hostile enforcement of rules
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between first-class service and coach hostility, but not exceptional comedy.

jokes.text: Use this one. I get frequent flyer miles with every purchase. Wait a minute, wait a minute. Get two tickets. You're returning it anyway, what's the difference? I'll get double the bonus miles.
jokes.setup: George giving credit card for ticket
jokes.punchline: Escalating from helping to exploiting the situation for miles
jokes.explanation: Perfect George opportunism - turning favor into personal gain through twisted logic.

jokes.text: Are you sure? Yes, I'm sure. I would know if a tray of food had been served to me. Would you?
jokes.setup: Elaine claiming she didn't get meal
jokes.punchline: Flight attendant questioning Elaine's awareness of being fed
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist hostility - questioning someone's ability to know if they've eaten.

jokes.text: No, no, it all has to do with the way they kill the pig. Come on, but they don't eat pigs. They do if it's killed right. Under a rabbi's supervision.
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong explanation of kosher dietary laws
jokes.explanation: Good ignorance humor - confident but completely wrong explanation of religious practice.

jokes.text: This is the best sundae I've ever had. Oh, man. You know what? They got the fudge on the bottom. You see? That enables you to control your fudge distribution...as you're eating your ice cream.
jokes.punchline: Detailed analysis of fudge placement engineering
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry overanalysis of mundane details. 'Fudge distribution control' is perfectly absurd technical language.

jokes.text: I never met a man who knew so much about nothing. Thank you.
jokes.punchline: Perfect description of Jerry/show's premise, which Jerry accepts as compliment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on the show itself. Jerry accepting this as a compliment is perfect character moment and series summation.

jokes.text: Supersavers? Are they refundable? You bought nonrefundable tickets. You idiot.
jokes.setup: Kramer bought tickets to catch Grossbard
jokes.punchline: George's horror at financial implications
jokes.explanation: George's financial anxiety taking precedence over friendship is in character but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Do you recognize me? No. Come on. Twenty years ago. What? Eighteenth Street? I don't know what you're talking about. Give me your money. Where's your wallet? The $240.
jokes.punchline: Immediate escalation to demanding money from confused stranger
jokes.explanation: Kramer's aggressive approach to debt collection is in character. Creates tension more than laughs.

jokes.text: No. Please don't send me back there. I'll do anything. It's so nice up here. It's so comfortable up here. I don't wanna go back there. Please, don't send me back there.
jokes.punchline: Desperate begging to stay in first class
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy as Elaine's dignity crumbles. The repetitive begging works for embarrassment factor.

jokes.text: You know, our goal should be a society without classes.
jokes.setup: Elaine being escorted back to coach
jokes.punchline: Sudden political philosophy to justify her behavior
jokes.explanation: Excellent rationalization humor - Elaine turning personal embarrassment into social justice cause.

jokes.text: Do you realize the people up here are getting cookies?!
jokes.punchline: Treating cookie inequality as grave injustice
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - cookies become symbol of class warfare. Elaine's outrage is proportionally absurd.

jokes.text: The problem is that curtain is no security. There really should be a locking door.
jokes.punchline: Suggesting prison-like security for airplane seating
jokes.explanation: Jerry's entitled first-class attitude perfectly captured in security suggestion. Shows his character transformation.

jokes.text: That's why they call it 'standby.' You end up standing there going, 'Bye. I didn't... Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing bit about standby flying
jokes.punchline: Literal interpretation of 'standby' as waving goodbye
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry wordplay in stand-up closing. Not exceptional but solid observational structure.

jokes.text: 'Would you bring your seatback all the way up?' 'Who the hell are you?' She says, 'I'm the flight attendant.' 'Yeah, well, then I'm the pilot, all right? So why don't you sit down? I'm about to bring her in.'
jokes.punchline: Passenger's sarcastic response claiming to be pilot
jokes.explanation: Strong closing bit with good escalation and dialogue. 'I'm about to bring her in' is a nice specific aviation detail.
